serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of cheese omelette in Singapore is approximately 250 g, typically presented as a folded egg omelette filled with melted cheese, served on a plate with a side salad or toast.

nutrient_source.title: Cheese Omelette — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for energy (307.0 kcal), protein (16.09 g), fat (23.06 g), carbohydrate (8.85 g), sugar (6.24 g), sodium (1279.0 mg), dietary fibre (0.0 g), potassium (295.0 mg), calcium (1375.0 mg), magnesium (34.0 mg), iron (0.95 mg), and cholesterol (78.0 mg) are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Cheese Omelette (confidence 0.95). Saturated fat (6.0 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Egg, whole, cooked, omelet' (FDC ID 173424) combined with cheddar cheese (FDC ID 328637) in approximate proportions consistent with a cheese omelette; trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for butter and cheddar cheese; vitamin C (0.0 mg/100 g) is confirmed negligible from USDA FoodData Central for eggs and cheese — no vitamin-C-contributing ingredients are present in standard preparation. GrabFood is not used as a nutrient source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Foods: a physiological basis for carbohydrate exchange — Jenkins et al. (1981), American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; supplemented by Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journals)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Cheese omelette is an egg-and-cheese dish with 8.85 g carbohydrate per 100 g, of which 6.24 g is sugar. Eggs and cheese are established low-GI foods with GI values near zero in peer-reviewed GI literature (Jenkins et al. 1981; Atkinson et al. 2008). Because the carbohydrate content is negligible and the food is dominated by protein and fat, the glycemic index is not applicable for this food. glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to 'not_applicable' per the critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1279 mg per 100 g — more than double the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit cheese omelette in its standard form; sodium reduction requires omitting processed cheese and ham, using reduced-sodium cheese, and replacing added salt with herbs and black pepper.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (8.85 g/100 g) and high protein (16.09 g/100 g) support glycaemic stability, but saturated fat at 6.0 g/100 g may impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cheese omelette can be included in a Type 2 Diabetes meal plan due to its low carbohydrate and negligible glycaemic load, but saturated fat intake should be moderated by using reduced-fat cheese and replacing butter with a small amount of olive oil.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1279 mg/100 g), saturated fat (6.0 g/100 g), trans fat (0.2 g/100 g), and cholesterol (78 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit cheese omelette due to its high sodium, saturated fat, and trans fat content; if consumed, portion size should be small, processed cheese and ham should be omitted, and butter replaced with olive oil to reduce atherogenic fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (6.0 g/100 g) from butter and cheese, combined with high sodium (1279 mg/100 g), promotes systemic inflammation and tissue oedema relevant to RA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise cheese omelette consumption in its standard form; replacing butter with extra-virgin olive oil, using reduced-fat cheese, and omitting processed meats will lower the pro-inflammatory fat and sodium burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese omelette is a pan-fried egg-based dish prepared by whisking whole eggs, pouring them into a buttered or oiled frying pan, and folding the partially set egg around a filling of melted cheese — commonly cheddar, processed cheese slices, or a blend of cheeses. In Singapore, it is a popular hawker centre and café item served at breakfast, lunch, or dinner, often accompanied by toast, a side salad, or plain rice. The texture is soft and custardy on the inside with a lightly golden exterior; the flavour is rich, savoury, and mildly salty from the cheese. Per 100 g, the dish provides 307 kcal, 16.09 g protein, 23.06 g fat (of which 6.0 g is saturated), 8.85 g carbohydrate, and a notably high sodium content of 1279 mg, largely attributable to the cheese and any added salt. Calcium is exceptionally high at 1375 mg/100 g due to the cheese content. The dish is gluten-free in its basic form. Clinically, the high sodium makes it unsuitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management without significant modification; the low carbohydrate and high protein profile make it relatively favourable for blood glucose control in Type 2 Diabetes, though saturated fat should be monitored. The glycaemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates relative to its protein and fat content. A standard Singapore serving is approximately 250 g, yielding approximately 768 kcal and 3198 mg sodium per plate.

Is cheese omelette good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and most micronutrients are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (confidence 0.95) for an exact match on Cheese Omelette. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked egg omelette and cheddar cheese, which are approved secondary sources. GI is not applicable for this low-carbohydrate food per peer-reviewed GI literature. Overall record confidence is rated high due to the primary HPB source exact match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for cheese omelette is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific composite dish as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and are not available from a single approved source for this exact composite preparation as of 2026-04-16. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source — eggs and cheese contain negligible dietary fibre. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for eggs and cheese; no vitamin-C-contributing ingredients are present in standard preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.2 g per 100 g: estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for butter and cheddar cheese. saturated_fat_g is 6.0 g per 100 g: estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked egg omelette (FDC ID 173424) and cheddar cheese (FDC ID 328637). Calcium value of 1375 mg/100 g is unusually high and reflects the HPB source value; this may indicate a cheese-heavy formulation or a data anomaly — flagged for downstream review. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic_index set to null and gi_category to 'not_applicable' per critical rule q4. Cultural classification: the input record lists primary_cuisine as Indian; however, cheese omelette is a broadly international egg dish widely consumed across Singapore's multicultural food landscape and is not exclusively Indian in origin — cuisine is recorded as Singaporean / International with SG region tag preserved.
